elements.sent-2411-001: In dynamic vehicle routing problems (DVRPs), some part of the information is revealed or changed on the fly, and the decision maker has the opportunity to re-plan the vehicle routes during their execution, reflecting on the changes.
elements.sent-2411-002: Accordingly, the solution to a DVRP is a flexible policy rather than a set of fixed routes.
elements.sent-2411-003: A policy is basically a problem-specific algorithm that is invoked at various decision points in the planning horizon and returns a decision according to the current state.
elements.sent-2411-004: Since DVRPs involve dynamic decision making, a simulator is an essential tool for dynamically testing and evaluating the policies.
elements.sent-2411-005: Despite this, there are few tools available that are specifically designed for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-006: To fill this gap, we have developed a simulation framework that is suitable for a wide range of dynamic vehicle routing problems and allows to dynamically test different policies for the given problem.
elements.sent-2411-007: In this paper, we present the background of this simulation tool, for which we proposed a general modeling framework suitable for formalizing DVRPs independently of simulation purposes.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our open source simulation tool is already available, easy to use, and easily customizable, making it a useful tool for the research community.
elements.sent-2411-009: Keywords: dynamic vehicle routing; modeling framework; simulation framework; discrete-event based decision process.
elements.sent-2411-010: A vehicle routing problem is dynamic, if some part of the information is revealed or changed on the fly, and the decision maker (the service provider) has the opportunity to re-plan the vehicle routes during their execution, reflecting on the changes.
elements.sent-2411-011: Dynamic vehicle routing problems (DVRPs) have received a lot of attention in the past decades, which is certified by a series of recent review papers, e.g., \citet{berbeglia2010dynamic, pillac2013review, bekta2014chapter, psaraftis2016dynamic, ritzinger2016survey, rios2021recent, soeffker2022stochastic, zhang2023dynamic, mardevsic2023review .}
elements.sent-2411-012: This growing interest is due to the wide range of real-world applications and the fact that today's technology enables real-time decision making.
elements.sent-2411-013: Nowadays, DVRPs are usually modeled using the so-called sequential decision process (e.g., \citet{ulmer2020modeling, soeffker2022stochastic ).}
elements.sent-2411-014: Briefly stated, the decision process transitions from decision point to decision point, where the decision maker is provided with the current state (i.e., all the available information) and has the opportunity to make a decision (e.g., update the vehicle routes), or in other words, to choose an action, see \Cref{fig:sdp:sdp .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Accordingly, a solution to the dynamic problem is a policy, which is a function that assigns an action to every state.
elements.sent-2411-016: Apart from survey articles, in the majority of the papers dealing with DVRPs, the authors propose policies for the problem at hand, and perform computational experiments to evaluate them, e.g., to compare them with state-of-the-art or baseline policies.
elements.sent-2411-017: In addition to doing the obviously necessary implementation of their policy, they need some kind of simulator for dynamic evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper, we focus on this dynamic evaluation, and we approach the DVRPs from the simulation point of view.
elements.sent-2411-019: Even more emphasized, our focus is not on the solution approaches for a particular DVRP, but on the modeling of general problems and on the dynamic testing of arbitrary solution methods.
elements.sent-2411-020: According to our primary goal, we have implemented a simulation framework that is suitable for a wide range of dynamic vehicle routing problems and allows to dynamically test different solution approaches for the modeled problem.
elements.sent-2411-021: This article, however, is much more than technical documentation, as we also propose a general modeling framework suitable for formalizing DVRPs independently of simulation purposes.
elements.sent-2411-022: The simulation of the decision process is essential for the dynamic evaluation of solution approaches to dynamic vehicle routing problems.
elements.sent-2411-023: In simpler cases, it is very easy to implement the sequential decision process, since the transition between the states is straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-024: However, in many other cases (especially when inter-route constraints make the problem difficult), it is necessary to run a more complex simulation to move the decision process from decision point to decision point.
elements.sent-2411-025: Although general-purpose simulation tools exist (e.g., AnyLogic, SimPy), they require the user to build the entire dynamic vehicle routing framework from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-026: Several publicly available simulators have been created using these tools, but they are only suitable for a specific problem (see e.g., \citet{hao2022introduction ).}
elements.sent-2411-027: Transportation simulation software packages (e.g., Eclipse SUMO, MATSim, PTV Vissim, Transims) could potentially support dynamic testing, but most of these tools focus primarily on microscopic traffic simulation (including elements such as traffic lights and pedestrian interactions), a level of detail that is rarely considered for research in our scope.
elements.sent-2411-028: We would like to highlight the work of \citet{maciejewski2016dynamic, maciejewski2017towards , where the authors developed a DVRP extension for MATSim.}
elements.sent-2411-029: This extension allows the modeling of a wide variety of DVRPs and the plugging of different algorithms, therefore this tool is indeed suitable for dynamical testing.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, modeling and customization requires familiarity with Java and the relatively complex architecture of MATSim, including a batch of scenario files.
elements.sent-2411-031: Our understanding is that the implementation of the decision making algorithm is also tied to Java.
elements.sent-2411-032: Based on the above, it is a reasonable goal to develop a standalone simulation tool for DVRPs according to the following criteria.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our main goal was to develop a general simulation tool for dynamic vehicle routing.
elements.sent-2411-034: To achieve this, we conducted an extensive literature review and developed a general modeling and simulation framework.
elements.sent-2411-035: Our main contributions are the following.
elements.sent-2411-036: We studied the literature on dynamic vehicle routing to identify those problem aspects and side constraints that are common and should therefore be considered in the development of the framework.
elements.paragraph-2411-002: Modeling and simulation framework for DVRPs
elements.sent-2411-038: We developed a general modeling and simulation framework for dynamic vehicle routing.
elements.sent-2411-039: The framework is suitable for modeling a wide range of DVRPs, primarily pickup-and-delivery problems, but it is easily adaptable to other problems as well.
elements.sent-2411-040: Our discrete-event based decision process is a combination of the discrete-event based simulation and the sequential decision process, the latter of which is widely used to formalize DVRPs.
elements.sent-2411-041: For the modeling, we borrowed the route-based representation of \citet{ulmer2020modeling , but we propose a more detailed model suitable for simulation purposes, see \Cref{fig:sdp:dep .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: We also standardized and formalized some common aspects of decision making, such as postponing decisions and delaying the departure of vehicles.
elements.paragraph-2411-003: Open source simulation tool for DVRPs
elements.sent-2411-044: According to our primary goal, we created an implementation of our simulation framework.
elements.sent-2411-045: The source code of our Python package, called dvrpsim, is available online.
elements.sent-2411-046: Dynamic vehicle routing problems can be easily modeled, and the simulator is easily customizable, making it a useful tool for other researchers to dynamically test and evaluate their algorithms for a particular problem.
elements.sent-2411-047: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first simulation tool designed specifically for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-049: In this section, we provide a brief introduction to dynamic vehicle routing.
elements.sent-2411-050: We also summarize our literature review on dynamic vehicle routing problems.
elements.sent-2411-051: We compiled the reviewed papers in \Cref{tab:lit:vrpdsr,tab:lit:dpdp,tab:lit:sddp .}
elements.sent-2411-052: The goal of the review was to identify those problem aspects and side constraints that often occur in the literature, therefore, they should be taken into account when developing a general modeling and simulation framework.
elements.sent-2411-053: As the focus is on modeling and simulation, the literature review does not cover problem aspects such as logistic context, objective functions, solution approaches, etc. For such an overview, we refer to the excellent review by \citet{zhang2023dynamic .}
elements.sent-2411-054: Briefly stated, the well-known (static) vehicle routing problem (VRP) aims to determine an optimal set of routes to be performed by a fleet of vehicles to fulfill order requests at different locations within a planning horizon.
elements.sent-2411-055: The problem was introduced more than 60 years ago by \citet{dantzig1959truck , then generalized by \citet{clarke1964scheduling , and many variations have appeared since then (e.g., \citet{toth2002vehicle, eksioglu2009vehicle, braekers2016vehicle, zhang2022review ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: According to \citet{psaraftis1980dynamic , a vehicle routing problem is characterized as dynamic, if the input of the problem is received and updated concurrently with the determination of the routes.}
elements.sent-2411-057: The vehicle routes can be redefined in an ongoing fashion.
elements.sent-2411-058: This class of problems is often referred to as online or real-time.
elements.sent-2411-059: Using the taxonomy of \citet{pillac2013review , a dynamic problem is stochastic, if there is some exploitable stochastic knowledge about the dynamically revealed information, and deterministic otherwise.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Thus, stochastic dynamic vehicle routing problems (SDVRPs) are also within the scope of our paper.
elements.sent-2411-061: In a recent survey, \citet{zhang2023dynamic considered three DVRP subcategories by distinguishing three types of order requests.}
elements.sent-2411-062: See \Cref{tab:lit:sddp for our summary on the related same-day delivery problems (SDDPs).}
elements.sent-2411-063: See \Cref{tab:lit:vrpdsr for our overview on vehicle routing problems with dynamic service requests (VRPDSRs).}
elements.sent-2411-064: In this paper, we focus on the three DVRP subcategories considered by \citet{zhang2023dynamic .}
elements.sent-2411-065: We present our modeling framework primarily for DPDPs (including SDDPs) as we assume that each request has a designated origin and a designated destination, however, with a slight modification the framework is also adaptable to DVRPs with service requests.
elements.sent-2411-066: Note that \citet{zhang2023dynamic identified another DVRP variant in addition to the previous ones, called the dynamic multi-period VRP (DMPVRP), which is characterized by multiple planning periods.}
elements.sent-2411-067: In this paper, we do not consider these problems.
elements.sent-2411-068: We also do not consider those problems, where the transportation consists of multiple stages, such as multi-echelon vehicle routing or vehicle routing with transshipment.
elements.sent-2411-069: For a review on these problems, see e.g., \citet{sluijk2023two, nielsen2024systematic .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Nowadays, the state-of-the-art approach to modeling DVRPs is the sequential (or Markov) decision process.
elements.sent-2411-071: For a thorough introduction, see \citep{ulmer2020modeling, soeffker2022stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-072: Briefly stated, at certain time points in the planning horizon, called decision points, the decision maker has the opportunity to re-plan the vehicle routes, reflecting on the newly revealed information, see \Cref{fig:sdp:sdp .}
elements.sent-2411-073: These decision points may be predetermined (e.g., they occur at given intervals), or they can be imposed by certain events (e.g., requesting an order).
elements.sent-2411-074: The sequential decision process steps from decision point to decision point, called transition.
elements.sent-2411-075: At a decision point, the decision maker is provided with the current state, which describes all the information available to make a decision.
elements.sent-2411-076: The result of the decision making is an action that includes, for example, the updated vehicle routes.
elements.sent-2411-077: Note in advance that our discrete-event based decision process differs from the sequential decision process in that it explicitly considers events between decision points, see \Cref{fig:sdp:dep .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Besides the fact that this approach makes it easier to formalize the dynamic problem in some cases, this level of detail allows us to construct a general, easily customizable simulation framework.
elements.sent-2411-079: Now, we present the main aspects and side constraints of dynamic vehicle routing problems that were considered when building our framework.
elements.sent-2411-080: We group these aspects by locations (\Cref{sec:bas:prob:locs ), orders (\Cref{sec:bas:prob:ords ), and vehicles (\Cref{sec:bas:prob:vehs ), but there may be some overlap between the groups.}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Location is a collective term for the places that vehicles may visit, such as depots, customers, restaurants, factories, etc., depending on the problem at hand.
elements.sent-2411-082: At this level of logistics planning, vehicles operate on networks.
elements.sent-2411-083: That is, the movement of vehicles is not detailed; they are either at a location (residing at a network node) or on the way (traveling along a network edge).
elements.sent-2411-084: In the latter case, the exact positions of the vehicles are unknown, but their arrival can be calculated from the travel time.
elements.sent-2411-085: In certain cases, vehicle movements are simulated within a real-world road network, such that road crossings also refer to locations (e.g., \citet{ferrucci2014realtime, ferrucci2015general, ferrucci2016proactive ).}
elements.sent-2411-086: Vehicles, especially if they are different types (e.g. drones and trucks), can operate on different networks (e.g., \citet{ulmer2018sameday ).}
elements.sent-2411-087: Travel times between locations can be vehicle-dependent, for example, if vehicles have different speeds, and especially if the vehicles operate on different networks (e.g., \citet{ulmer2018sameday ).}
elements.sent-2411-088: Travel times can also be time-dependent (e.g., \citet{haghani2005dynamic ) or even stochastic (e.g., \citet{schilde2014integrating ).}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Locations often have limited space for loading or unloading, and sometimes the loading crew creates a bottleneck.
elements.sent-2411-090: Because of these inter-route constraints, vehicles may make each other wait.
elements.sent-2411-091: For example, \citet{hao2022introduction proposed a problem, where each factory has a limited number of docking ports, so if a vehicle arrives and there is no port available, the vehicle must wait until a port becomes available.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Orders are transportation or service requests.
elements.sent-2411-093: The object of transportation can be a variety of products, food (e.g., meal delivery problem), other vehicles (e.g., bike sharing rebalancing problem), or even people (e.g., dial-a-ride problem).
elements.sent-2411-094: Transportation requests typically have an origin (i.e., pickup location) and a destination (i.e., delivery location).
elements.sent-2411-095: In many cases, the terms "order" and "customer" are used interchangeably.
elements.sent-2411-096: Various service times may arise when orders are picked up or delivered.
elements.sent-2411-097: Loading and unloading itself may take some time and may even depend on the quantity of orders (e.g., \citet{hao2022introduction ).}
elements.sent-2411-098: These times can also be location-dependent (e.g., \citet{ulmer2019preemptive ) or vehicle-dependent (e.g., \citet{ulmer2018sameday ).}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Additional order-independent service times, such as parking or docking, may also occur (e.g., \citet{hao2022introduction ).}
elements.sent-2411-100: The above service times can even be stochastic (e.g., \citet{goel2019vehicle ), but in many cases they are simply neglected or incorporated in the travel times.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Orders often have service time windows for their pickup and/or delivery.
elements.sent-2411-102: Such a time window specifies an earliest and a latest service start time for the order.
elements.sent-2411-103: Earliest service start times are typically hard constraints, meaning that if a vehicle arrives early at a location, it has to wait until the time window opens, but \citet{schilde2014integrating , for example, allowed early arrivals.}
elements.sent-2411-104: In contrast, latest service start times are often soft constraints, that is, the service can start after the latest required time, however, the tardiness may incur additional costs (e.g., \citet{ulmer2021restaurant ).}
elements.sent-2411-105: In some rare cases, customers have multiple time windows in the planning horizon (e.g., \citet{de2015constrained, de2015variable ).}
elements.sent-2411-106: Service time windows can be stochastic.
elements.sent-2411-107: For example, in the problem proposed by \citet{srour2018strategies , customers first preannounce their request with an estimated time window for pickup, which can be changed when the customer confirms the request.}
elements.sent-2411-108: In some cases, customers can cancel their requests (e.g., \citet{lin2014decision, los2020value ).}
elements.sent-2411-109: Cancellation is allowed only if the service of the corresponding order has not yet started.
elements.sent-2411-110: After the notification, the decision maker must remove the canceled orders from the vehicle routes.
elements.sent-2411-111: Cancellation is permanent, and canceled orders are no longer dealt with in the given planning horizon.
elements.sent-2411-112: Vehicle is a collective term for the equipment or people that perform the transportation, such as trucks, drones, drivers, couriers, etc., depending on the problem at hand.
elements.sent-2411-113: The fleet of vehicles can be either homogeneous or heterogeneous.
elements.sent-2411-114: In the latter case, vehicles may differ not only in their basic parameters, but also in their operations.
elements.sent-2411-115: For example, \citet{ulmer2018sameday considered a problem with heterogeneous fleets of drones and trucks that differ not only in their availability, capacity, and travel speed, but also in their requirement for charging and the network on which they operate.}
elements.sent-2411-116: A vehicle is either capacitated or uncapacitated.
elements.sent-2411-117: In the former case, the total size or quantity of orders loaded on the vehicle must never exceed the capacity of the vehicle.
elements.sent-2411-118: In dial-a-ride or taxi-routing problems, the capacity of the vehicles is the number of non-driver seats, however, in some cases no shared rides are allowed, that is, a vehicle can only carry one passenger (or one passenger group) at a time (e.g., \citet{hyland2018dynamic ).}
elements.sent-2411-119: The uncapacitated case is common with those problems where the packages are relatively small and therefore the trunk of the transporting vehicle is not a limiting factor.
elements.sent-2411-120: Vehicles can be subject to loading rules.
elements.sent-2411-121: For example, in \citep{hao2022introduction , unloading must follow the last-in-first-out (LIFO) rule, i.e., the last loaded order must be unloaded first.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Vehicles can also have time windows, representing the working shifts of the drivers (e.g., \citet{de2015variable, steever2019dynamic ).}
elements.sent-2411-123: Sometimes, a time window~$[0,L]$ is associated with the depot, also called the depot deadline, which gives a latest return time~($L$) for the vehicles (e.g., \citet{cote2023branch ).}
elements.sent-2411-124: Several questions may arise when making decisions.
elements.sent-2411-125: When or how often is it necessary to re-optimize (\Cref{sec:int:dec:points )? Can and should an order be rejected (\Cref{sec:int:dec:rejection )? Should all decisions be taken as soon as possible, or can certain decisions be postponed (\Cref{sec:int:dec:opp )? Should vehicles be sent on their way immediately or is it worth waiting (\Cref{sec:int:dec:delay )? Can en route vehicles be diverted or should their destination not be changed (\Cref{sec:int:dec:enroute )? Can a request be served by multiple routes (\Cref{sec:int:dec:split )?.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-126: In the case of DVRPs, the decision maker must decide when to process the new dynamic information and update the routes of the vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-127: Most of the articles use three different approaches, namely the decision maker makes a decision either periodically, when a new order request arrives, or when a vehicle arrives at a location, however, there are several other possibilities, and the various approaches can also be combined.
elements.sent-2411-128: In many applications, the planning horizon is divided into predetermined decision epochs, typically of equal length ($\Delta$), i.e., decision points occur periodically.
elements.sent-2411-129: For example, \citet{zolfagharinia2014benefit re-planned truck routes twice a day ($\Delta = 12\,h$).}
elements.sent-2411-130: In the framework proposed by \citet{hao2022introduction for a dynamic pickup-and-delivery problem, information is updated every 10 minutes ($\Delta = 10\,min$).}
elements.sent-2411-131: The most common case is that decisions are made when new orders are requested.
elements.sent-2411-132: Often, a decision point is imposed when a vehicle arrives at a location.
elements.sent-2411-133: In some cases, complete order information is not available until arrival, so routes may need to be re-planned prior to the start of service (e.g., \citet{goodson2016restockingbased ).}
elements.sent-2411-134: For some same-day delivery problems, the planned vehicle routes are fixed, so re-optimization occurs only when a vehicle returns to the depot (e.g., \citet{dayarian2020sameday ).}
elements.sent-2411-135: In fact, most of the cases decision making is required after the service is finished, but since service times are neglected, it coincides with the arrival.
elements.sent-2411-136: In many approaches, the planned route of a vehicle consists only of the next location to visit, so it is necessary to re-plan the route after the service is finished (e.g., \citet{ulmer2018budgeting, ulmer2019offlineonline ).}
elements.sent-2411-137: In some cases, certain decisions can be postponed, which often involves the introduction of self-imposed decision points.
elements.sent-2411-138: That is, if no other event imposes a decision point by a certain time point, then reaching that time will impose one to reconsider the decision.
elements.sent-2411-139: For example, \citet{zhang2018dynamic considered an orienteering problem in which a traveler must join a waiting queue upon arrival at a location.}
elements.sent-2411-140: If the traveler joins the queue, the next decision point is imposed when the size of the queue decreases or a predetermined maximum waiting time elapses, whichever occurs first.
elements.sent-2411-141: Thus, the authors proposed a policy, where the assignment of some non-urgent orders is postponed for a given unit of time, and if no new orders are requested during this period, the expiration of the postponement imposes a decision point.
elements.sent-2411-142: In certain cases, delaying the departure of the vehicles can also cause self-imposed decision points, see later in \Cref{sec:int:dec:delay .}
elements.sent-2411-143: In many applications, the decision maker can reject orders, if they are unable or unwilling to fulfill them.
elements.sent-2411-144: The rejection is permanent, and rejected orders are no longer dealt with in the given planning horizon.
elements.sent-2411-145: In practice, rejected orders may be outsourced to a third party or moved to another planning horizon.
elements.sent-2411-146: In the problem proposed by \citet{ehmke2014customer , the decision maker allows the customer to request an alternative order with a different time window, if the original order is rejected.}
elements.sent-2411-147: As we touched on in \Cref{sec:int:dec:points , certain decisions can be postponed in some cases.}
elements.sent-2411-148: In our interpretation, decision postponement means that certain non-changeable decisions are not made at the current decision point, but are postponed to a later one.
elements.sent-2411-149: For example, if order rejection is allowed, the acceptance/rejection is permanent, therefore some authors do not want to make the decision at the first possible decision point (e.g., \citet{zhang2018dynamic, voccia2019sameday ).}
elements.sent-2411-150: Sometimes, the assignment of orders to vehicles, once made, cannot be altered, so the decision on this assignment is postponed (e.g., \citet{ulmer2021restaurant ).}
elements.sent-2411-151: Note that the case where the order requests do not impose decision points, and the orders are accepted or rejected at the first decision point after their request, is not considered as decision postponement.
elements.sent-2411-152: In addition to assigning routes to vehicles, it is also important to decide when to send vehicles on their way, since waiting for possible future orders could be beneficial.
elements.sent-2411-153: The two basic waiting strategies, the drive-first and the wait-first, require a vehicle to departure from its current location at the earliest possible time and at the latest possible time, respectively, but several other waiting strategies have been applied to delay the departure of the vehicles (e.g., \citet{mitrovic2004waiting, branke2005waiting, ichoua2006exploiting ).}
elements.sent-2411-154: As mentioned in \Cref{sec:int:dec:points , delaying the departure may involve the use of self-imposed decision points.}
elements.sent-2411-155: For example, \citet{voccia2019sameday considered a same-day delivery problem, where the depot-to-depot tours cannot be modified during their execution.}
elements.sent-2411-156: In their policy, the authors did not start the vehicles immediately after determining their routes, but postponed them for a certain period of time.
elements.sent-2411-157: A decision point was implied at the end of the waiting period, unless another event triggered one in the meantime.
elements.sent-2411-158: Due to the dynamic nature of the problem, the decision maker may modify the vehicle routes during execution.
elements.sent-2411-159: Although the majority of papers consider decision making to be instantaneous, in practice it may cover longer periods of time during which the state of the system may change so much (e.g., some vehicles may have already departed) that the decision is no longer feasible with respect to this new state.
elements.sent-2411-160: Therefore, it may be advisable to fix the first parts of the routes, i.e. to make them non-changeable.
elements.sent-2411-161: In most SDDPs, once the vehicle leaves the depot, its entire route is fixed until it returns to the depot.
elements.sent-2411-162: In some other cases, however, a preemptive depot return is allowed, that is, the delivery vehicle can return to the depot before delivering all the orders it is currently carrying (e.g., \citet{ulmer2019preemptive, cote2023branch ).}
elements.sent-2411-163: In general, the next location of a vehicle is fixed.
elements.sent-2411-164: This is especially true when the vehicle is already en route.
elements.sent-2411-165: In some rare cases, however, researchers enable en route diversion (e.g., \citet{ulmer2017value, bosse2023dynamic ).}
elements.sent-2411-166: In some other cases, vehicle movements are simulated within a real-world road network, where turning on the street is not allowed, so diversions from the current route can only take place at the next road crossing (e.g., \citet{ferrucci2014realtime, ferrucci2015general, ferrucci2016proactive ).}
elements.sent-2411-167: Since in these problems, the road crossings can also be modeled as locations, we do not consider this approach as an en route diversion.
elements.sent-2411-168: In a similar approach, \citet{haferkamp2024design considered those locations to be deviation points that were located on a traveled shortest path.}
elements.sent-2411-169: Split delivery means that a single request can be served by multiple vehicles (or multiple routes of the same vehicle).

elements.section-2411-003: A general modeling framework for dynamic vehicle routing I. - Basic concepts
elements.sent-2411-172: In this section, we propose the basic concept and terminology of our modeling framework.
elements.sent-2411-173: First, we provide an overview of the problems under investigation (\Cref{sec:bas:overview ).}
elements.sent-2411-174: Then, we discuss the main elements in detail, which are the locations (\Cref{sec:bas:locations ), the orders (\Cref{sec:bas:orders ), and the vehicles (\Cref{sec:bas:vehicles ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-175: A heterogeneous fleet of vehicles must serve pickup-and-delivery type orders that arrive dynamically in the planning horizon.
elements.sent-2411-176: The pickup/delivery locations can refer to a designated depot, so our modeling framework is suitable for modeling not only DPDPs, but also SDDPs.
elements.sent-2411-177: Various VRPDSRs can be modeled, for example, by specifying coincident pickup and delivery locations.
elements.sent-2411-178: Due to the dynamic nature of the problem, the decision maker has the opportunity to re-plan the vehicle routes at certain decision points.
elements.sent-2411-179: Decision points may be imposed by arbitrary events (e.g., on order request, on vehicle arrival) or may occur periodically.
elements.sent-2411-180: Any parameter of the problem (e.g., request of orders, travel times) can be deterministic or stochastic.
elements.sent-2411-181: A service time window can be associated with both the pickup and the delivery of the orders.
elements.sent-2411-182: Both cancellation by the customers and rejection by the decision maker can be handled.
elements.sent-2411-183: In the latter case, the postponement of the decision on acceptance/rejection is also allowed.
elements.sent-2411-184: Split deliveries are allowed, but in this case, the orders must be split into the smallest deliverable units in advance.
elements.sent-2411-185: It is the decision maker's responsibility to combine and assign them to vehicles according to the splitting rules.
elements.sent-2411-186: Vehicles can be capacitated or uncapacitated, and may be subject to loading rules.
elements.sent-2411-188: The planned routes of the vehicles can be modified during their execution, however, en route diversion is not allowed.
elements.sent-2411-189: Locations may have limited docking capacity, so the vehicles may have to wait for service.
elements.sent-2411-190: Certain aspects of the problem (\Cref{sec:bas:problem ) and the decisions (\Cref{sec:bas:decision ) are not necessarily subject to simulation, but rather to decision making.}}

elements.section-2411-005: An open source simulation tool for dynamic vehicle routing
elements.sent-2411-337: In this section, we briefly present the main components of our simulation framework for dynamic vehicle routing, called dvrpsim.
elements.sent-2411-338: Our goal is to provide a concise overview of how to use the simulation package.
elements.sent-2411-339: For an extended, technical description, we refer to \Cref{sec:supp .}
elements.sent-2411-340: A more detailed tutorial can be found on the webpage of the package: \url{https://sztaki-hu.github.io/dvrpsim/ .}
elements.sent-2411-341: Our simulator is implemented in Python language, however, the implementation of the decision making procedure (also called external routing algorithm) is not tied to Python.
elements.sent-2411-342: For the implementation, we used the SimPy package\footnote{\url{https://simpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ , which is a single-thread process-based discrete-event simulation framework.}}
elements.sent-2411-343: The source code is available at \url{https://github.com/sztaki-hu/dvrpsim .}
elements.sent-2411-344: Assuming Python is already installed, the package can also be installed by typing python -m pip install dvrpsim at the command prompt.
elements.sent-2411-345: To model a vehicle routing problem, the user needs to build a Model, and to add the necessary Locations, Orders, and Vehicles that represent the corresponding locations, orders, and vehicles, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-346: These classes have several callback methods, which can be customized to model their desired behavior.
elements.sent-2411-347: The routing callback of the Model must be also implemented to connect the external routing algorithm and the simulator.
elements.sent-2411-348: By starting the simulation (i) each order is requested at its release time; (ii) when a decision point is imposed, the external routing algorithm is called; (iii) once a route plan is set for a vehicle, it begins to execute it.
elements.sent-2411-349: Unless the user implements otherwise, the simulation ends when all orders have been processed (i.e., delivered, canceled, or rejected).
elements.sent-2411-350: At the end of the simulation, the history of the vehicles and orders is available, thus various statistics can be generated.
elements.sent-2411-351: Each Location can optionally be associated with coordinates and a shared resource to model its capacity.
elements.sent-2411-352: The distances and travel times between the locations can be defined and/or used in the corresponding callbacks of the Vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-353: Each Order must be associated with a release time, a pickup location, and a delivery location.
elements.sent-2411-354: There are also several other optional parameters (such as quantity, pickup/delivery time window, pickup/delivery duration, etc.).
elements.sent-2411-355: During the simulation, each order is requested at its release time, after which the order is available for insertion into a vehicle route.
elements.sent-2411-356: Note that orders can also be created on the fly, while the simulation is running.
elements.sent-2411-357: An Order has several callback methods that are invoked, for example, when the order is requested, rejected, canceled, postponed, picked up, delivered, or when the postponement of the order is expired.
elements.sent-2411-358: By requesting routing in such a callback, the user can model, for example, decision points on order request/cancellation/postponement.
elements.sent-2411-359: Each Vehicle must be associated with an initial location, and there are several other optional parameters (such as capacity, loading rule, etc.).
elements.sent-2411-360: In addition, the travel time callback should be defined that returns the travel time for the vehicle between the corresponding locations.
elements.sent-2411-361: During the simulation, once a route plan is set for a vehicle as a result of decision making, the vehicle begins to execute it.
elements.sent-2411-362: Recall that the execution procedure of a vehicle consists of four main parts, these are, the pre-departure, the travel, the pre-service, and the service (see \Cref{fig:mod:veh:exe ).}
elements.sent-2411-363: By default, the pre-departure procedure delays the departure of the vehicle when an earliest start time is associated with the next visit.
elements.sent-2411-364: The travel procedure uses the travel time callback to obtain the arrival time at the next location.
elements.sent-2411-365: The pre-service procedure takes into account the earliest service start times of the corresponding orders and the capacity of the corresponding location and, if necessary, makes the vehicle wait accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-366: The service procedure models the unloading and the loading of the corresponding orders.
elements.sent-2411-367: A Vehicle have several callback methods that are invoked, for example, when the vehicle arrives/departs at/from a location, when the service of the vehicle starts/finishes, or when one of its process is interrupted.
elements.sent-2411-368: By requesting routing in such a callback, we can model, for example, decision points on vehicle arrival.
elements.sent-2411-369: The routing callback of the Model can be used to connect the external routing algorithm and the simulator.
elements.sent-2411-370: The external routing algorithm can be implemented in arbitrary programming language.
elements.sent-2411-371: Note that the external routing algorithm does not have to be necessary "external", as the algorithm itself can also be implemented in that callback.
elements.sent-2411-372: At each decision point, a routing callback is invoked, which includes invoking the external routing algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-373: The simulator provides the current state in JSON format, allowing file-based interaction with the external routing algorithm, which is especially useful if the latter is not implemented in Python.
elements.sent-2411-374: The output of the routing algorithm (i.e., the decision) is processed and enforced.
elements.sent-2411-375: Before enforcing the decision, it is possible to check various problem constraints (e.g., the capacity constraints of the vehicles).
elements.sent-2411-376: By default, the simulator assumes instantaneous (i.e., zero time) decision making, but real-time decision making can also be modeled.
elements.sent-2411-377: As a proof-of-concept, we implemented several examples using our simulator, which are available together with the source code.
elements.sent-2411-378: The following three examples deal with three very different problems with very different problem aspects and constraints, demonstrating that the framework is suitable for modeling a wide range of dynamic vehicle routing problems.
elements.sent-2411-379: A dynamic pickup-and-delivery problem was introduced in a competition organized by the International Conference on Automated Planning and Scheduling in~2021 (ICAPS 2021), see \citep{hao2022introduction .}
elements.sent-2411-380: There is a fleet of homogeneous vehicles that has to serve pickup-and-delivery order requests which occur over a day.
elements.sent-2411-381: Each order is characterized by a quantity, a pickup factory, a delivery factory, a release time, and a due date.
elements.sent-2411-382: The vehicles can be loaded up to their capacity, while unloading has to follow the last-in-first-out (LIFO) rule.
elements.sent-2411-383: Those, but only those orders whose quantity exceeds the capacity of the vehicles, can be split and delivered separately.
elements.sent-2411-384: The travel times and the distances between the factories are given.
elements.sent-2411-385: Each factory has a given number of docking ports for serving (that is, loading and unloading) the vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-386: Vehicles are served on a first-come-first-served basis.
elements.sent-2411-387: If a vehicle arrives at a factory and all ports are occupied, its service cannot begin immediately, but the vehicle has to join the waiting queue.
elements.sent-2411-388: That is, the vehicle must wait until one of the docking ports becomes free, and no vehicle that arrived earlier is waiting for a port.
elements.sent-2411-389: The objective is to satisfy all the requests such that a combination of tardiness penalties and traveling distances is minimized.
elements.sent-2411-390: Decision points occur in every 10 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-391: To model this problem, we used the default Location class, where each location is associated with a shared resource to model the of its docking ports.
elements.sent-2411-392: We also used the default Order class.
elements.sent-2411-393: We inherited a custom Vehicle class, where (i)~the travel time callback returns the pre-given travel times; (ii)~the service procedure is extended to model dock approaching of the vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-394: Capacity and LIFO loading rule are also set for the vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-395: A pre-defined method is used to impose decision points in every 10 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-396: The form of states and decisions is also modified, so that the Model can be connected with the already implemented algorithms for the problem.
elements.sent-2411-397: The benchmark instances for their work are publicly available.
elements.sent-2411-398: The problem is characterized by a fleet of vehicles operating from a depot and by a set of locations.
elements.sent-2411-399: Customers request service throughout the day until a fixed cut-off time.
elements.sent-2411-400: Arrivals of requests are described by a known arrival rate and distribution.
elements.sent-2411-401: Associated with each request is a known service time and a delivery time window at the customer location.
elements.sent-2411-402: Once requests are made, a vehicle at the depot can be assigned requests and leave the depot immediately.
elements.sent-2411-403: Alternatively, a vehicle can wait at the depot before being assigned requests.
elements.sent-2411-404: Once a vehicle leaves the depot, the route for that vehicle is fixed, and the vehicle returns to the depot when it has made all its assigned deliveries.
elements.sent-2411-405: A request is assigned to a third party when it is no longer feasible for the request to be served by a vehicle at the depot or one of the vehicles en route.
elements.sent-2411-406: A decison point is imposed as a result of at least one of the following: (i)~a vehicle arrives at the depot; (ii)~a vehicle ends its waiting period; (iii)~a new request arrives and at least one vehicle is waiting at the depot.
elements.sent-2411-407: To model this problem, we used the default Location and Order classes.
elements.sent-2411-408: There is a location for the depot, and there is a separate location for each customer.
elements.sent-2411-409: Each location is associated with latitude and longitude coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-410: We inherited a custom Vehicle class, where the travel time callback returns the travel times calculated on Manhattan-distances.
elements.sent-2411-411: The 'on arrival' callback of the vehicles, and the 'on request' callback of the orders are customized to impose decision points on the appropriate events.
elements.sent-2411-412: Our demo routing algorithm sets earliest start time for the routes to delay the departure from the depot.
elements.sent-2411-413: The problem is characterized by a fleet of vehicles that seeks to fulfill a random set of delivery orders that arrive during the finite order horizon from restaurants located in a service area.
elements.sent-2411-414: Orders occur according to a known stochastic process.
elements.sent-2411-415: Each realized order is associated with an order time, a delivery location, a pickup restaurant, and a soft deadline.
elements.sent-2411-416: The time to prepare a customer’s food at each restaurant is random.
elements.sent-2411-417: Thus, the driver may need to wait for the order’s completion when arriving to a restaurant.
elements.sent-2411-418: The dispatcher determines which orders are assigned to which vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-419: Once made, assignments cannot be altered, therefore, assignments can be postponed.
elements.sent-2411-420: A decision point occurs when a new customer requests service.
elements.sent-2411-421: A decision point can also be self-imposed, which happens when an order is postponed.
elements.sent-2411-422: There is a separate location for each restaurant, each customer, and each vehicle.
elements.sent-2411-423: Decision points are imposed on order requests.
elements.sent-2411-424: We inherited a custom Vehicle class, where (i)~the travel time callback returns the travel times calculated on Euclidean-distances; (ii) the pre-service procedure of vehicles are customized to model stochastic ready times.
elements.sent-2411-425: In this paper, we focused on developing a simulation tool designed to model a wide range of dynamic vehicle routing problems (DVRPs) to support the dynamic testing of different solution methods.
elements.sent-2411-426: We began by conducting an extensive literature review to identify the key aspects and common constraints in DVRPs that should be considered in the modeling framework.
elements.sent-2411-427: Based on these findings, we developed a general modeling and simulation framework tailored for simulation purposes.
elements.sent-2411-428: Finally, we have created an implementation of the framework and made it freely available.
elements.sent-2411-429: As a proof-of-concept, we have implemented several examples with our framework.
elements.sent-2411-430: These case studies deal with different problems with very different problem aspects and constraints, demonstrating that the framework is suitable for modeling a wide range of dynamic vehicle routing problems.

elements.section-2411-010: Supplementary material: A general simulation framework for dynamic vehicle routing
elements.sent-2411-454: In this section, we take a closer look at the main components of our simulation framework.
elements.sent-2411-455: For the implementation of the simulator, we used the SimPy package\footnote{\url{https://simpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ , which is a single-thread process-based discrete-event simulation framework.}}
elements.sent-2411-456: This framework works with Processes, which can interact with each other via Events.
elements.sent-2411-457: A Process is basically a generator function.
elements.sent-2411-458: When a Process yields an Event, the Process is suspended, and the Event is scheduled, i.e., it is added to the event queue.
elements.sent-2411-459: The Process is resumed, when this Event is processed, i.e., it occurs.
elements.sent-2411-460: In the following, we will use several types of Events.
elements.sent-2411-461: The Event $\operatorname{event (t)$ will be processed at time~$t$.}
elements.sent-2411-462: The timeout Event $\operatorname{timeout (\delta)$ will be processed $\delta$ times later after it is scheduled.}
elements.sent-2411-463: The latter is equivalent with $\operatorname{event (\tnow + \delta)$, where $\tnow$ refers to the current simulation time.}
elements.sent-2411-464: The Event $\operatorname{allof (e_1,e_2,\ldots)$ will be processed when all of the Events $e_1,e_2,\ldots$ are processed.}
elements.sent-2411-466: When a Process is interrupted, the yielded Event is removed from the event queue, and the underlying function is terminated.
elements.sent-2411-467: Processes can be also interact with each other via shared resources.
elements.sent-2411-468: For example, a Resource is conceptually a semaphore with a given capacity.
elements.sent-2411-469: A request on a Resource yields an Event which will be processed, when a capacity is freed.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Routing procedure modeling instantaneous decision making}\label{alg:sim:routing} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Input $\varphi$: global boolean variable \State $\varphi \gets False$ \Yield $\operatorname{timeout}(0)$ with high priority \Comment{decision point} \If{$\varphi$ is True} \State\Return \EndIf \State $\varphi \gets True$ \State invoke callback on routing start \State invoke external routing algorithm \Yield $\operatorname{timeout}(0)$ with low priority \Comment{decision enforcement} \State invoke callback on routing finish \State enforce decision \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-470: As described in the previous sections, certain events can impose a decision point, and then the decision maker makes a decision, which can, for example, change the route plans.
elements.sent-2411-471: In this environment, this procedure is implemented in the routing procedure, see \Cref{alg:sim:routing , and we say that some events request routing.}
elements.sent-2411-472: Once a routing is requested, the routing procedure is added to the environment in a separate Process.
elements.sent-2411-473: The routing procedure consists of the following steps.
elements.sent-2411-474: A timeout Event -- which corresponds to a decision point event (see \Cref{sec:sdp:events ) -- is yielded with zero delay and high priority.}
elements.sent-2411-475: Because of this, those Events that also occur at that time, will be processed before this Event.
elements.sent-2411-476: Second, we can detect if multiple routing requests arrive at the same time (i.e., multiple routing Processes are added to the environment), thus we can achieve that only the first can proceed to Step~2, see flag~$\varphi$ in \Cref{alg:sim:routing .}
elements.sent-2411-477: A callback on routing start is invoked.
elements.sent-2411-478: By default, this procedure interrupts the postponement Processes of the orders and the vehicles, see later in \Cref{sec:sim:ord:opp,sec:sim:veh:predep .}
elements.sent-2411-479: The external routing algorithm is invoked.
elements.sent-2411-480: The external routing algorithm is provided with the current state of the model, then returns a decision on the vehicles and the orders.
elements.sent-2411-481: Finally, a timeout Event $\operatorname{timeout (\delta)$ -- which corresponds to a decision enforcement event described in \Cref{sec:sdp:events -- is yielded to indicate the end of the routing.}}
elements.sent-2411-482: By default, $\delta = 0$ regardless of the real run-time of the external routing algorithm, that is, the simulator models instantaneous decisions.
elements.sent-2411-483: If one wants to model real-time decisions, the delay must correspond to the run-time of the external routing algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-484: After the routing is finished, a callback is invoked, which can be used, for example, to check the feasibility of the obtained action (see \Cref{sec:apx:feas ).}
elements.sent-2411-485: The resulted decision is enforced, as described in \Cref{sec:sdp:act:pds .}
elements.sent-2411-486: If any state-feasibility constraints (\Cref{sec:sdp:act:feas ) is violated, the simulator terminates with error.}
elements.sent-2411-487: After the status of the orders are updated, the postponement procedure of postponed orders, if any, are started in separate Processes, see later in \Cref{sec:sim:ord:opp .}
elements.sent-2411-488: After the routes are updated, the execution procedure of idle vehicles, if any, are started in separate Processes, see later in \Cref{sec:sim:veh:predep .}
elements.sent-2411-489: Arbitrary processes can be added to the simulation environment.
elements.sent-2411-490: For example, the procedure depicted in \Cref{alg:sim:periodic can be added to impose decision points at given time steps.}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Procedure for periodic decision points}\label{alg:sim:periodic} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Input $\Delta$: epoch length \While{some not rejected orders are not delivered} \State request routing \Yield $\operatorname{timeout}(\Delta)$ \EndWhile \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-491: In the simulator, each order is modeled with an instance of the Order class.
elements.sent-2411-492: There are multiple ways in the simulator to request orders.
elements.sent-2411-493: For an example, in the procedure depicted in \Cref{alg:ord:or , the orders are sorted in ascending order based on their release time.}
elements.sent-2411-494: Then, for each order an Event is yielded that corresponds to an order request event described in \Cref{sec:sdp:events .}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{An order requesting procedure}\label{alg:ord:or} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $\mathcal{O} \gets $ orders sorted by their release time ($r_{\cdot}$) \For{order $o_i \in \mathcal{O}$} \State $\delta \gets r_i - \tnow$ \Yield $\operatorname{timeout}(\delta)$ \Comment{order request} \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-495: As described in \Cref{sec:bas:ord:post , our framework allows order postponement.}
elements.sent-2411-496: Recall that when a decision is enforced, a postponement Process is started separately for each postponed order, if any (see Step~5 in \Cref{sec:sim:routing ).}
elements.sent-2411-497: The postponement procedure of an order, see \Cref{alg:ord:opp , is a simple method that yields an Event with the time until which the order has been postponed.}
elements.sent-2411-498: If the postponement procedure is processed, a callback on order postponement expiration is invoked, requesting routing by default.
elements.sent-2411-499: Also recall that the postponement process will be interrupted if a routing is requested in the meantime (see Step~1 in \Cref{sec:sim:routing ).}
elements.sent-2411-500: These two cases correspond to Case~1 and Case~2 in \Cref{sec:bas:ord:post , respectively.}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Postponement procedure for orders (default)}\label{alg:ord:opp} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $t\gets$ the time until the order is postponed \If{ $\tnow < t$ } \Yield $\operatorname{event}(t)$ \Comment{order postponement expired} \EndIf \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-501: By requesting routing in such a callback, we can model, for example, decision points on order request/cancellation/postponement.
elements.sent-2411-502: Each vehicle is modeled with an instance of the Vehicle class.
elements.sent-2411-503: The execution procedure of a vehicle executes its route plan as described in \Cref{sec:bas:veh:exe .}
elements.sent-2411-504: This execution procedure consists of four consecutive parts, these are, the pre-departure procedure, the departure procedure, the pre-service procedure, and the service procedure, see \Cref{fig:sim:vexe .}
elements.sent-2411-505: After the route plans are updated during a routing procedure (\Cref{sec:sim:routing , Step~5), idle vehicles start their execution procedure (i.e., a procedure for each idle vehicle is added to the simulation environment in a separate Process).}
elements.sent-2411-506: The pre-departure procedure models the phase before departure.
elements.sent-2411-507: The Event indicating the end of the procedure corresponds to a vehicle departure event described in \Cref{sec:sdp:events .}
elements.sent-2411-508: The procedure is suitable, for example, to delay the departure of the vehicles.
elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{Pre-departure procedure (default)}\label{alg:veh:predep} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $t\gets$ earliest start time for the next visit (0, if not given) \If{$\tnow < t$} \Yield $\operatorname{event}(t)$ \Comment{departure postponement expired} \State request routing (optional) \EndIf \Yield $\operatorname{timeout}(0)$ \Comment{vehicle departure} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-509: The default pre-departure procedure, see \Cref{alg:veh:predep , checks whether an earliest start time that has not yet passed is associated with the vehicle's next visit, and if so, yields an Event with that time.}
elements.sent-2411-510: By this, the travel procedure of the vehicle will start at that earliest start time.
elements.sent-2411-511: Note, however, that the pre-departure procedure can be interrupted, as happens when a routing procedure is about to begin (\Cref{sec:sim:routing , Step~1).}
elements.sent-2411-512: This behavior is consistent with the procedure proposed in the modeling framework (\Cref{sec:bas:veh:del ).}
elements.sent-2411-513: The travel procedure models the travel of the vehicle from its current location to its next location to visit.
elements.sent-2411-514: By default, see \Cref{alg:veh:travel , the procedure gets the corresponding travel time, and yields a timeout Event with that delay.}
elements.sent-2411-515: This Event corresponds to a vehicle arrival event described in \Cref{sec:sdp:events .}
elements.sent-2411-516: The travel time procedure can be used, for example, to model deterministic, time-dependent, or stochastic travel times, etc..
elements.algorithm-2411-006: \caption{Travel procedure (default)}\label{alg:veh:travel} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $\delta\gets$ travel time from current location to next location \Yield $\operatorname{timeout}(\delta)$ \Comment{vehicle arrival} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-517: The travel time between the corresponding locations can be a pre-calculated constant value or can be calculated on the fly.
elements.sent-2411-518: Since the procedure has access to the current simulation time, it is easy to implement time-dependent travel times as well.
elements.sent-2411-519: The procedure is also suitable for implementing stochastic travel times, see \Cref{alg:stoch:travel for a primitive example.}
elements.algorithm-2411-007: \caption{Custom travel procedure with stochastic travel times}\label{alg:stoch:travel} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $\delta_1\gets$ travel time from current location to next location \Yield $\operatorname{timeout}(\delta_1)$ \State $\delta_2\gets$ random delay \Yield $\operatorname{timeout}(\delta_2)$ \Comment{vehicle arrival} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-520: Our modeling framework does not allow en route diversion, so travel Processes cannot be interrupted by default.
elements.sent-2411-521: However, all the necessary callbacks for this purpose are provided in our simulator.
elements.sent-2411-522: The pre-service procedure models the phase between an arrival and the start of the subsequent service.
elements.sent-2411-523: The Event indicating the end of the procedure corresponds to a service start event described in \Cref{sec:sdp:events .}
elements.sent-2411-524: The procedure can be used to delay the start of the service, and thus it is suitable, for example, for modeling earliest service start times, docking restrictions, stochastic completion times, etc..
elements.algorithm-2411-008: \caption{Pre-service procedure (default)}\label{alg:veh:preserv} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $t\gets$ latest earliest pickup/delivery service start time of orders to pickup/deliver \State $r\gets$ service request \Yield $\operatorname{allof}(\operatorname{event}(t),r)$ \Comment{service start} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-525: Orders can be associated with earliest pickup/delivery service start times (\Cref{sec:bas:orders ).}
elements.sent-2411-526: These are hard constraints, that is, even if the vehicle has arrived at the location, the service cannot start until the time window for each order tp deliver/pickup is opened.
elements.sent-2411-527: The default pre-service procedure, see \Cref{alg:veh:preserv , creates an Event that is processed at the latest earliest service start time, so sthat the service cannot start earlier.}
elements.sent-2411-528: Shared resources can be associated with the locations to limit the number of vehicles that can be served simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-529: For example, Resources can be used to model the number of docking ports at the locations.
elements.sent-2411-530: The default pre-service procedure, see \Cref{alg:veh:preserv , makes a request on the Resource of the corresponding location, so the service can only start after a docking port has become available for the vehicle.}
elements.sent-2411-531: In the restaurant meal delivery problem of \citep{ulmer2021restaurant , the completion times of the orders are stochastic, and the drivers may need to wait at restaurants for the order to be ready.}
elements.sent-2411-532: The custom pre-service procedure depicted in~\Cref{alg:rmdp:preserv models this behavior.}
elements.algorithm-2411-009: \caption{Custom pre-service procedure for stochastic ready times}\label{alg:rmdp:preserv} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $t\gets$ ready time of the order to pickup ($\tnow$, if it is ready) \Yield $\operatorname{event}(t)$ \Comment{service start} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-533: The service procedure models the service of the vehicle at its current location.
elements.sent-2411-534: The main purpose of the procedure is to model the pickup and the delivery of the orders, see \Cref{alg:veh:service , however, other vehicle activities (e.g., parking) can also be included.}
elements.sent-2411-535: The service procedure can be used, for example, to model deterministic service times, stochastic service times, order-independent service times, etc..
elements.algorithm-2411-010: \caption{Service procedure (default)}\label{alg:veh:service} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \For{order~$o_i$ to deliver} \State $\delta_d\gets$ delivery service time of the order \label{alg:veh:service:dstime} \Yield $\operatorname{timeout}(\delta_d)$ \Comment{order delivery} \EndFor \For{order~$o_i$ to pickup} \State $\delta_p\gets$ pickup service time of the order \label{alg:veh:service:pstime} \Yield $\operatorname{timeout}(\delta_p)$ \Comment{order pickup} \EndFor \Yield $\operatorname{timeout}(0)$ \Comment{service finish} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-536: In the default service procedure, see \Cref{alg:veh:service , unloading takes place first and then loading takes place afterwards.}
elements.sent-2411-537: Each pickup/delivery is modeled with a timeout Event with a delay corresponding to the duration of the pickup/delivery.
elements.sent-2411-538: That service times can be either deterministic or stochastic, and can depend on the order, on the vehicle, and on the location as well.
elements.sent-2411-539: Order-independent service times (e.g., parking, docking) can be also taken into consideration.
elements.sent-2411-540: For example, in case of the dynamic pickup and delivery problem of \citet{hao2022introduction , the services start with a fixed-length docking, which can be modeled by the procedure depicted in \Cref{alg:dpdp:service .}}
elements.algorithm-2411-011: \caption{Custom service procedure}\label{alg:dpdp:service} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $\delta\gets$ dock approaching time \Yield $\operatorname{timeout}(\delta)$ \State apply default service procedure \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-541: Vehicles have several callback methods, which are invoked, for example, when the vehicle arrives/departs at/from a location, when the service of the vehicle starts/finishes, or when one of its process is interrupted.
